data.title: BCA’s Berezin: Stocks to go lower, ‘open to doubling down’ on profitable bearish SPY bet
data.description:
Peter Berezin at BCA Research said a put option bet made a hefty profit with his projection that stocks were vulnerable to a selloff, and he told Seeking Alpha another bearish bet may be placed as the S&P 500 (SP500) has further to fall to hit his below-consensus target of 4,450. 

BCA’s chief global strategist late Monday told clients it was closing its long SPY June 30, 2025 $550 put recommendation after making a gain of 536%. Profit arrived through the mauling in equities (SP500) stemming from tariff worries, a selloff Berezin foresaw in December when establishing BCA’s S&P 500 (SP500) target. 

“Yesterday, stocks had fallen a lot, so we decided to book those profits,” Berezin told Seeking Alpha in an interview Tuesday. “I'm open to doubling down again on that trade because there's still downside for stocks and the economy.” 

The S&P 500 (SP500) was ~5,040 on Tuesday, with a stock rally faltering after U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer said President Donald Trump won’t grant tariff exemptions on goods from global trading partners. 

“I guess investors are thinking that we're going to strike great deals with everyone else. But the problem is that the U.S. trade deficit is not because of the tariffs that other countries charge the U.S.,” Berezin said.

“The U.S. trade deficit exists because other countries often prefer to buy U.S. assets rather than U.S. goods. They'd rather buy Treasuries or Nvidia (NVDA)stock because they see the U.S. as a safe and attractive place in which to invest. That's a good thing,” Berezin said. “So Trump's whole tariff policy is just illogical.” 

Trump’s large-scale tariffs have spurred economists from Goldman Sachs and other Wall Street brokerages to warn about rising recession risks. Meanwhile, the White House on Tuesday said Trump will raise levies on Chinese goods to 104%, effective Wednesday, because China hasn’t rescinded its own retaliatory tariffs on U.S. goods. 

“We're heading into a recession, and the market is not trading at levels consistent with a recession. Corporate spreads are not levels consistent with recession. Commodity prices are not consistent with levels of a recession, so there's more downside risk for a lot of these cyclical assets,” Berezin said. 

Keeping in mind there will be periods of stock rebounds, BCA may initiate a new trade through puts or “just an outright short,” Berezin said. “But my target remains - it’s still 4,450 on the S&P 500 (SP500).”

data.title: FDIC to ease banks' living will rules, acting chair says
data.description:
In coming days, the Federal Insurance Deposit Corp. ("FDIC") will waive several requirements in the plans banks are required to draw up for an orderly wind-down, should a bank fail, what are commonly called living wills, the acting chair said on Tuesday.

He thinks it's useless to focus resolution planning around entering and exiting a bridge bank. Hill says current rules ignore the lesson of the 2023 episode of banking turmoil triggered by the failures of Silicon Valley Bank and Signature Bank. The key lesson of those failures is "how costly a bridge bank solution can be," he said. 

"In particular, we will deemphasize and broaden the 'strategy' discussion and waive the expectation that banks identify and build their plans around a hypothetical failure scenario," said Travis Hill, acting chair of the FDIC, at the American Bankers Association Washington Summit  "Instead, we will look for plans focused more specifically on providing the FDIC the information it needs to rapidly market the institution and, if needed, operate the institution for a short period of time."

"A secondary objective should be to be prepared to operate the institution for a short period of time post failure, while pursuing a sale of at least the deposit franchise as quickly as possible," Hill said.

The FDIC plans to reach out to large financial institutions in their capacity as potential acquirers. "Our motivation is to be as prepared as possible to rapidly market a large, failing institution with little advanced notice, so we are not flat-footed, as we were with SVB," he said.

Specifically, the FDIC should ask such prospective acquirers:

  • What information and data do bidders need?
  • How should the information and data be presented?
  • What type of transaction structures are more or less attractive?
  • Is the documentation sufficiently clear?
  • How else can the FDIC improve the bidding process?

The FDIC also intends to engage with potential non-bank bidders to facilitate their ability both to partner with banks on bids and to bid individually on particular asset pools.

"Overall, we hope to be much better prepared in the event that a large bank fails in the future, and to be able to resolve it in a much more cost-effective manner," he said.

data.title: Guggenheim stays bullish on Meta on user engagement strength
data.description:
• Guggenheim maintained its investment rating of "buy" on Meta (NASDAQ:META) on Tuesday and noted that user engagement on its platforms remains a strength for the company.
  • The research firm said despite macro uncertainty, Meta remains the top destination for incremental ad dollars.
  • They also expect Meta to see further efficiency gains through growing deployment of Advantage+.
  • Reels "remains another focus area" and is being seen as the primary driver of impression and engagement growth in the near term, Guggenheim said.
  • The research firm forecasts total advertising revenue growth of +14.1% in Q1 and does not expect the company to temper the investment roadmap based on macro softness.
  • It also anticipates continued headcount growth in priority areas such as Gen AI.
  • META's PT was cut to $675 from $750, implying an upside of 30.8%.

data.title: Fidelity National, First American, PennyMac raised at KBW on upside potential after sell-off
data.description:
Title insurers Fidelity National Financial (NYSE:FNF) and First American Financial (NYSE:FAF) and mortgage servicer PennyMac Financial Services (NYSE:PFSI) were upgraded at Keefe, Bruyette & Woods on upside potential following the market sell-off.

"We are upgrading the larger-cap title insurers (FNF/FAF) to Outperform as we find the current entry points attractive given we now see +23% and +33% upside to our respective price targets following the -13% pullback in both FNF and FAF shares since 4/2," said the equity research firm in a note.

"While the weakness in the title shares is likely due to concerns regarding the CRE (commercial real estate) market, we think the lower rate environment and the benefit it could have on residential mortgage volumes could act as an offset to any economic slowdown," said the note.

FNF is down ~5% in the last one month, while FAF has dipped ~7.5%.

The stocks were raised to Outperform from Market Perform, along with PennyMac Financial, citing servicing earnings should be strong again in Q1.

"We are anticipating another strong quarter of servicing earnings in Q1, driven by prepayment speeds that returned to historically low levels in Q1 despite interest/mortgage rates declining ~25-30 bp over the course of the quarter," according to the research note.

PennyMac Financial gets a Buy rating from the Wall Street community and a Sell rating from Quant. Meanwhile, Quant assigns a Buy rating to Fidelity National and Hold rating to First American Financial. The sell-side analysts rate both FNF and FAF as Buy.

data.title: Alcoa cut to Sell equivalent at BofA on caution over aluminum, alumina prices
data.description:
Alcoa (NYSE:AA) -6.3% in Tuesday's trading as Bank of America double downgrades shares to Underperform from Buy with a $26 price target, slashed from $58, driven by lower earnings estimates due to a weaker outlook for aluminum and alumina prices and growing uncertainty around demand into 2025 and 2026.

BofA analyst Lawson Winder says Alcoa (NYSE:AA) has significant gearing to the prices of both commodities, and he now expects lower average aluminum and alumina prices to drag on earnings, with forecast EBITDA for 2025 cut by 46% to $1.55B and free cash flow cut by 68% to $313M; the bank's revised estimates also show net debt rising to $1.05B by the end of 2025, more than doubling from a previous forecast.

With 70% of Alcoa's (AA) Canadian production exported to the U.S., Winder sees valuation downside risk should a falling U.S. Midwest premium reduce the potential offset to tariffs on Alcoa's (AA) Canadian aluminum imports to the U.S., and should global energy prices continue falling, declining aluminum/alumina cost curves might pressure prices lower.

Winder also notes recent softness in the U.S. Midwest Premium, a key price offset, increases the potential earnings drag from Section 232 tariffs; if the premium remains flat from late November levels, the analyst estimates an annual negative EBITDA impact of $411M.

data.title: Volatility grips Wall Street again as major averages seesaw
data.description:
Another topsy-turvy trading session on Wall Street saw the major averages rally furiously at the open, then steadily give up gains and turn negative, after which they are fluctuating. 

A U.S. government official doused hopes of any tariff exemptions and a Federal Reserve speaker largely spoke negatively about the trade barriers. Meanwhile, White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt said tariffs will go into effect even as negotiations continue.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last +0.20% at 5,072.44 points in late afternoon trade, having earlier hit as much as +4% immediately after the opening bell. It also slipped as much as -0.40%.

 The tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +0.12% at 15,622.72 points, giving up gains of as much as +4.5%. and falling as much as -0.60%. The blue-chip Dow (DJI) was +0.62% at 38,258.54 points.

Over in the fixed-income markets, a selloff in bonds extended from Monday and pushed U.S. Treasury yields higher. According to Apollo Asset Management, the selling may be linked to the unwinding of the "basis trade." The 10-year yield (US10Y) was last up 5 basis points to 4.20%. The shorter-end more rate-sensitive 2-year yield (US2Y) was down 1 basis point to 3.73%.  

For more, see how Treasury yields have done across the curve on the Seeking Alpha bond page. 

U.S. Trade Representative ambassador Jamieson Greer said that President Trump had been clear that he won't issue any tariff exemptions any time soon. Greer's comments came in a U.S. Senate hearing. 

Chicago Fed president Austan Goolsbee on public radio said Trump's tariffs were way bigger than anticipated, adding that the central bank couldn't just rely on hard data to gauge the impact of the tariffs.   

Earlier, hopes of a trade deal had helped shore up sentiment following a historic few days of market volatility. Trump announced his sweeping reciprocal tariffs - the steepest American trade barriers in over a century - last Wednesday. What followed was carnage as markets across the world plummeted on fears of a global trade war destroying economic growth.

The S&P 500 (SP500) cratered more than -10% over last Thursday and Friday, one of its biggest two-day declines in four decades. Investors then returned from the weekend and proceeded to whipsaw through a topsy-turvy Monday. The S&P swung from one extreme to another, gaining as much as +3.40% and also losing as much as -4.71%. The benchmark index briefly swung into bear market territory.

"The stock market briefly touched bear market territory on Monday, and for right now, we're still within the bounds of a correction and not a deeper bear market, even though this past week has been extremely uncomfortable," Clark Bellin, president and chief investment officer at Bellwether Wealth, said.  

"While there may be more downside ahead, we remind investors that there have been many examples of corrections that see a near 20% peak to trough decline, such as in 2018 and in 1998, only to see a steep rally soon after that is sustained for some time," he said. 

"Regardless of whether or not we see a recession, the stock market will at some point price-in the recovery. The stock market moves ahead of economic activity, and this is an important reminder for those who fear continued stock market downside if we see a recession materialize," Bellin added. 

Market participants this week will have plenty to look forward to, with upcoming economic data on inflation, several Federal Reserve speakers, and the start of the first quarter earnings season.

data.title: Carvana plans Phoenix, AZ auction and reconditioning megasite
data.description:
Carvana (NYSE:CVNA) plans to build an auction and reconditioning “Megasite” in Phoenix, Arizona, enabling the car dealership to expand its reconditioning capabilities with increased inventory, allowing local buyers to receive delivery of a vehicle on the same or next day.

Transitioning the Phoenix ADESA site to an auction-Inspection and Reconditioning Center (“IRC”) Megasite will be enabled by the site’s significant existing footprint and infrastructure. The site spans nearly 100 acres.

"Bringing Carvana Inspection and Reconditioning Center capabilities to ADESA Phoenix will drive additional speed and selection for our local retail customers and an even more robust offering for our local wholesale customers while also creating new entry level and skilled jobs in the Chandler community," Carvana Senior VP Brian Boyd said.  

Carvana (NYSE:CVNA) shares are up 8.6%.

data.title: UnitedHealth, Humana, CVS Health jump after Medicare rate decision
data.description:
Managed care firms UnitedHealth (NYSE:UNH), Humana (NYSE:HUM), and CVS Health (NYSE:CVS) led the gainers in the S&P 500 on Tuesday after the U.S. Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services finalized a favorable rate decision for Medicare Advantage plans for next year.

Other notable gainers include Alignment Healthcare (ALHC), Elevance Health (ELV), Clover Health (CLOV), and Centene (CNC), which also offer MA plans, the private versions of federally backed health coverage.

According to the CMS, the government payments to Medicare Advantage plans will rise roughly 5% for the 2026 calendar year, a sharp increase from the 2.23% the agency proposed as part of its Advance Notice in January.

Many Wall Street analysts argued that with the rate increase exceeding their expectations, shares of MA-focused name could rise amid recent market volatility.

“While improvement relative to the Advanced Notice was expected, we view the +283bp improvement as ahead of expectations,” Truist analyst David MacDonald said, according to Bloomberg News.

“We look for the better-than-expected MA Final Rate coupled with what we view as the sector’s attractive relative positioning in a volatile market backdrop to drive strength,” MacDonald added.

Mizuho analyst Ann Hynes argued that the difference between the proposed and finalized rates well surpassed Street’s expectations and could improve investor sentiment surrounding managed care firms.

“Given how difficult the Medicare Advantage regulatory environment was over the past three years under former President Biden’s administration, this improvement under President Trump should be a meaningful positive sentiment driver for the group,” Hynes added.
